title: What the July Award Increase Does to a Recruitment Business That Hasn't Repriced
linkedin:
Every 1 July, award wages increase. Most recruitment business owners know this is coming.

Far fewer have run the maths through the full on-cost stack before it happens.

The common mistake is modelling the award increase against the base rate only. The full picture costs more.

Super is calculated on ordinary time earnings, so it moves with the base rate. Shift allowances expressed as a percentage of ordinary time also compound upward. The aggregate impact on total hourly on-cost is consistently larger than the headline percentage when you look at the base rate alone.

If your charge rate agreements haven't been reviewed against that full calculation, you're entering July with margins already lower than you think.

There are two types of agreements at play. Some index to award movements automatically. Others require written notice before you can pass through a rate increase. If you haven't checked which of your active temp book contracts fall into which category, the notice window may already have closed for some of them.

That matters if you're carrying locked charge rates through a period of confirmed cost increases.

The EOFY review that actually serves a recruitment business isn't a compliance exercise. It's a contract-by-contract look at which relationships can absorb the July change, which need a rate conversation before Monday, and which will create a margin compression period to plan for across the next six weeks.

Layer in the receivables picture, and you have the July view. June invoices on 45-day terms don't settle until mid-August. The first July payroll runs in the opening week of the month.

That gap is a known, plannable risk. If you haven't mapped it yet, you have a few days.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

facebook:
For recruitment and labour hire businesses, the July award wage increase doesn't just affect what workers are paid. It affects the margin on every temp placement from the very first shift of the new financial year.

The common mistake is calculating the impact against the base rate only. Once super and shift allowances are factored in, the real cost increase is usually higher than the headline percentage suggests.

Have you checked your client charge rate agreements against the new July costs yet? Some agreements index automatically. Others need written notice before you can pass through a change.

With EOFY on Monday, that review is worth doing this weekend.

#AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
google:
If you run a recruitment or labour hire business in Queensland, New South Wales, or Victoria, the start of July brings a direct impact on your temp placement margins.

When the Fair Work Commission's annual wage decision takes effect from the first full pay period of July, the award base rate for most classifications increases. For an on-hire workforce, this also increases superannuation obligations and certain shift allowances, because both are calculated as a percentage of the base rate. The total on-cost increase per hour is typically larger than the headline rate percentage alone implies.

The issue is that charge rates don't update automatically. Whether you can pass through the increase to a client depends on your rate agreement. Some agreements index to award changes by default. Others require written notice before the new rate can apply. If that notice hasn't been sent, the window may already be closing for some agreements.

With the financial year ending on Monday 30 June, the most useful action a recruitment business can take right now is a contract-by-contract review of the active temp book. The goal is to identify which agreements can absorb the July change, which need a rate conversation with the client before the year closes, and which will create a known margin compression period in the early weeks of the new financial year.

This is a manageable and plannable issue. The businesses that work through it before June 30 enter July with a clear picture.

Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
